message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 19 (19 todo)
Recent changes: Added 20% loyalty discount on policy base premium when yearsWithMHPCO >= 2, applied before fee.

Refactor while keeping tests green. Consider extracting named constants (loyalty rate 0.2, threshold 2) and possibly a policy-wide-modifier structure since first-insurance (+10%) and follow-up (-15%) are coming next and apply to the policy base premium. Evaluate naming, duplication, APP — but don't add untested behavior. Document.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 19 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing the names in `quote`:

- `quote` — clearly reveals intent (produce a premium quote). Keep.
- `componentBasePremium` — sum of component premiums. Clear.
- `mainItemsPremium` — sum of non-component item premiums. Clear.
- `policyBasePremium` — combined base before policy-wide modifiers/fee. Clear.
- `loyaltyDiscount` — the discount amount. Clear.
- `itemPremium`, `componentGroupPremium`, `basePremiumFor`, `isComponent` — all reveal intent well.

Constants `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (2) and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (0.2) were already extracted in the green phase. **Naming is already good — no rename needed.**

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (the `quote` function body)

```
const componentBasePremium = COMPONENT_TYPES.reduce((sum, type) => {     loop:5, bindings sum+type:2, assign:6
    const count = items.filter((item)=>item.type===type).length;          assign:6, invoke filter:2 +.length:2, binding item:1, cond ===:4
    return sum + componentGroupPremium(type, count);                       invoke +:2, invoke componentGroupPremium:2
  }, 0);                                                                    const 0:1

const mainItemsPremium = items                                             assign:6
    .filter((item) => !isComponent(item))                                  invoke filter:2, binding item:1, invoke isComponent:2, invoke !:2
    .reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemPremium(item), 0);                    invoke reduce:2, bindings sum+item:2, invoke +:2, invoke itemPremium:2, const 0:1

const policyBasePremium = mainItemsPremium + componentBasePremium;         assign:6, invoke +:2, bindings:2
const loyaltyDiscount =                                                    assign:6
    context.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD                       cond >=:4, bindings (context.yearsWithMHPCO, THRESHOLD):2
      ? policyBasePremium * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;                      ternary cond:4, invoke *:2, bindings:2, const 0:1

return policyBasePremium - loyaltyDiscount + POLICY_FEE;                    invoke -:2, invoke +:2, bindings:3
```

Approximate total mass of `quote` body: **~96**. The exact number matters less than the relative before/after comparison.

## Step 3-4: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 19 green. Confirmed.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The loyalty discount is currently inline arithmetic. The prompt hints that first-insurance (+10%) and follow-up (-15%) modifiers are coming next, and all three apply to `policyBasePremium`. However, per the guidelines I must **not add untested behavior** — I cannot build a speculative policy-wide-modifier structure for tests that don't exist yet (that would be premature abstraction, a Rule 4 violation and a TDD failure mode).

What I *can* do without speculation: extract the loyalty calculation into a named helper. This improves intent-revealing (Rule 2) and isolates the single tested policy-wide modifier behind a clear name, which is a natural seam for future modifiers without inventing their structure now.

**Rule 3 (Duplication):** The two `reduce` blocks both sum premiums but over genuinely different domains (grouped components vs per-item mains). Collapsing them would hurt clarity (Rule 2 trumps). No real duplication to remove.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unnecessary abstraction present.

The cleanest, non-speculative improvement: extract a `loyaltyDiscountFor` helper. This names the policy-wide modifier behavior that is actually tested.

content.text:
No build script exists; vitest does type-aware transforms and all tests pass green, confirming compilation.

## Step 5: New APP Mass

By extracting `loyaltyDiscountFor`, the `quote` body shed the inline `loyaltyDiscount` assignment (assign:6) and its ternary/comparison/multiply chain (~15 mass), replaced by a single invocation (invoke:2 + bindings:2 = ~4). The extracted helper carries that logic at the same intrinsic mass it had before. So total program mass is roughly unchanged (~+1 for the new binding parameter), but mass is **redistributed out of the `quote` orchestrator** into a focused, named function — the `quote` body dropped from ~96 to ~81.

This is the correct APP trade-off: Rule 2 (reveals intent) takes priority over raw mass minimization, and the localized mass of `quote` is meaningfully lower.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Naming (Rule 2): Evaluated all names first — `quote`, `policyBasePremium`, `mainItemsPremium`, `componentBasePremium`, and the existing constants already revealed intent. No renames needed.
- Extract Function (Rule 2): Pulled the inline loyalty-discount arithmetic into `loyaltyDiscountFor(policyBasePremium, context)`. The `quote` function now reads as a clear pipeline: compute base premiums, subtract the named policy-wide discount, add fee.

**Deliberately NOT done (avoiding untested behavior):**
- The prompt suggested a possible policy-wide-modifier structure for the upcoming first-insurance (+10%) and follow-up (-15%) modifiers. I did **not** build it — those tests are still `it.todo()`. Building a generic modifier pipeline now would be premature abstraction (Rule 4 violation / TDD failure mode "implementing beyond what tests demand"). The new `loyaltyDiscountFor` helper is a natural, honest seam: when the next tests go red, the modifiers can be introduced incrementally without speculation.
- Named constants for the loyalty rate (0.2) and threshold (2) were already extracted in the green phase, so that suggested improvement was already satisfied.

**Rules check:**
- Rule 1 (Tests pass): 19/19 green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): Improved — loyalty modifier now has a name.
- Rule 3 (No duplication): The two `reduce` sums operate on distinct domains; merging would hurt clarity. None removed.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): No unnecessary elements added; declined the speculative structure.

## Step 7: Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted inline loyalty-discount logic into a named `loyaltyDiscountFor` helper, revealing the policy-wide-modifier intent. Names and rate/threshold constants were already clean.
- **Mass Change**: `quote` body localized mass reduced (~96 → ~81); total program mass roughly unchanged. Rule 2 (clarity) prioritized over raw mass.
- **Tests**: All 19 passing ✅ (19 todo remain).

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test (`should apply 10% first-insurance surcharge to the policy base premium`).
